hook: India just bet ₹40,000 crore that PCBs will be Made in India.
headline: ECMS Budget Doubles — PCB Manufacturing's Moment
body: The Union Budget 2026-27 just dropped a bombshell for Indian electronics manufacturing: the Electronics Components Manufacturing Scheme (ECMS) outlay has been hiked to ₹40,000 crore — a 75% jump from its original ₹22,919 crore. This isn't incremental policy. This is India going all-in on building a domestic PCB and components ecosystem.

The numbers back up the ambition. Investment commitments under ECMS have already hit ₹1.15 lakh crore — nearly double the original target. Forty-six projects across 11 states are approved, covering multi-layer PCBs, camera modules, connectors, optical transceivers, and more. Projected production over six years: over ₹10.3 lakh crore. Direct jobs: 1.4 lakh and counting.

For startups and hardware companies building in India, this changes the math on domestic sourcing. Until now, high-layer-count PCBs meant looking at China, Taiwan, or Thailand. But with this kind of government backing, domestic fab capacity for 8+ layer boards is finally scaling up. Chennai, Bangalore, and Hyderabad are emerging as serious PCB manufacturing corridors.

We started CU Circuits because we believed Indian fab could compete on quality, not just cost. This budget tells us the government agrees — and is putting serious money behind that belief. The USD 500-billion electronics manufacturing ecosystem India is targeting by 2030-31 needs world-class PCB fabs. We're building one.
insight: India's ECMS budget hike to ₹40,000 crore signals a structural shift — domestic multi-layer PCB capacity is scaling, and hardware startups should start qualifying Indian fab partners now before demand outpaces supply.
caption:
₹40,000 crore. That's not a typo — that's India's new bet on domestic electronics component manufacturing.

The ECMS outlay just got a 75% boost in the Union Budget, and multi-layer PCBs are right at the center of it. Investment commitments have already crossed ₹1.15 lakh crore.

At CU Circuits, this is the moment we've been building toward. Indian PCB fab isn't just catching up — it's gearing up to compete globally.

Are you sourcing PCBs domestically yet? If not, what's holding you back? Let's talk about it.
